- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

No file matching `libgross*` or `*gross*` exists in the workspace. 

Did you mean a different name? Candidates I can check:
- `libgloss` (newlib's bare-metal syscall stubs) - common in RISC-V bare-metal projects
- Something else specific to vsim-demo?

Please confirm the filename.

file.content:
#ifndef HTIF_H
#define HTIF_H

#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdint.h>

// L1 Cache Control Commands
#define CCTL_L1D_WBINVAL_ALL 6
#define CCTL_L1D_WB_ALL      7
#define CCTL_L1D_INVAL_ALL   23

// L2 Cache Control and Status Constants
#define CCTL_L2_WBINVAL_ALL  0x12
#define CCTL_L2_ST_RUNNING   1
#define CCTL_ST_MASK         0xF

// L2 Cache Control Register Addresses
#define L2C_REG_CTL          0xe0500008
#define L2C_REG_CCTL         0xe0500040
#define L2C_REG_CCTL_M0_ST   0xe0500080
#define TOHOST_CMD_REG       0xe0080010

#if __riscv_xlen == 64
#define TOHOST_CMD(dev, cmd, payload) \
  (((uint64_t)(dev) << 56) | ((uint64_t)(cmd) << 48) | (uint64_t)(payload))
#define REG_T uint64_t
#else
#define TOHOST_CMD(dev, cmd, payload) \
  ({ \
    if ((dev) || (cmd)) \
      __builtin_trap(); \
    (payload); \
  })
#define REG_T uint32_t
#endif

static inline void flush_all_dcache(void)
{
  // L1 DCache writeback and invalidate all
  asm volatile("csrw mcctlcommand, %0" :: "rK"(CCTL_L1D_WBINVAL_ALL));

#if __riscv_xlen == 64
  // Check for L2 cache and flush it if available
  asm volatile(
    "csrr    t0, mmsc_cfg\n"
    "li      t1, 1\n"
    "slli    t1, t1, 46\n"
    "and     t2, t0, t1\n"
    "beqz    t2, 1f\n"
    "li      t3, 0xe0500008\n"
    "ld      t4, 0(t3)\n"
    "andi    t4, t4, 1\n"
    "beqz    t4, 1f\n"
    "li      t5, 0xe0500040\n"
    "li      t6, %[cmd]\n"
    "sd      t6, 0(t5)\n"
    "2:\n"
    "li      t2, 0xe0500080\n"
    "ld      t3, 0(t2)\n"
    "li      t4, %[mask]\n"
    "and     t3, t3, t4\n"
    "li      t5, %[running]\n"
    "beq     t3, t5, 2b\n"
    "1:\n"
    :
    : [cmd] "K"(CCTL_L2_WBINVAL_ALL),
      [mask] "K"(CCTL_ST_MASK),
      [running] "K"(CCTL_L2_ST_RUNNING)
  );
#endif
}

__attribute__((used)) static inline void sim_syscall(uint64_t dev,
                                                       uint64_t cmd,
                                                       uint64_t tohost)
{
  volatile uint64_t* command = (volatile uint64_t*) TOHOST_CMD_REG;
  uint64_t tohost_cmd = TOHOST_CMD(dev, cmd, tohost);
  flush_all_dcache();
  *command = tohost_cmd;
  asm volatile("fence rw,rw");
}

__attribute__((used)) static inline uint64_t frontend_syscall(uint64_t n,
                                                               uint64_t a0,
                                                               uint64_t a1,
                                                               uint64_t a2,
                                                               uint64_t a3,
                                                               uint64_t a4,
                                                               uint64_t a5,
                                                               uint64_t a6)
{
  volatile uint64_t magicmem[8] = {n, a0, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6};
  sim_syscall(0, 0, (uintptr_t) magicmem);
  return magicmem[0];
}

#endif // HTIF_H

content.text:
vsim-demo links a custom `libgloss.c` because it runs bare-metal RISC-V binaries on the Verilator simulator, with no OS, but newlib's libc still calls syscall stubs (`_write`, `_read`, `_open`, `_exit`, `_sbrk`...). The stock libgloss that ships with the toolchain targets a generic semihosting/ECALL interface that vsim does not implement. This file replaces those stubs with vsim's specific host-target conventions:

1. **HTIF magic-memory protocol** (`frontend_syscall` in `htif.h`) for filesystem calls. Args are packed into an 8-word `magicmem` array and the address is posted to `TOHOST_CMD_REG` (0xe0080010); the simulator's sim_control TLM module reads the args from guest memory and executes the syscall on the host.

2. **MMIO display device** at `DISPLAY_BASE_ADDR` 0xe0080004 - `_write` to fd<=2 bytes-bangs to that address, which the simulator prints as stdout/stderr. Avoids a full HTIF round-trip per character.

3. **MMIO exit register** at `EXIT_COMMAND_ADDR` 0xe0080000 - `_exit` stores the status there to terminate the simulation cleanly, then spins. Without this the simulator has no way to know the program finished.

4. **Linker-symbol `_sbrk`** using `_end` from the linker script for the heap (newlib has no kernel to ask for memory).

5. **L1/L2 cache writeback** before every HTIF doorbell (`flush_all_dcache` via `mcctlcommand` CSR + L2 CCTL at 0xe0500040). The host side of HTIF reads guest DRAM directly through the TLM bus, so dirty lines stuck in DCache would be invisible to the simulator - this is the part you cannot get from generic libgloss.

In short, it is the glue mapping newlib POSIX syscalls onto vsim's HTIF + MMIO device contract.

lastPrompt: why does vsim implement a generic semihosting/ECALL interface?

content.text:
Confirmed by reading the source - vsim does **not** implement RISC-V semihosting or trap ECALL. The design is intentional:

**What vsim actually implements** (`vsim/src/platform/sim_control.hpp`):
- An MMIO TLM target at `region.base_address` (the `0xe0080000` region newlib's libgloss writes to)
- HTIF (Host-Target Interface, Berkeley/Spike convention) at `+0x10` - guest writes a 64-bit "tohost" packet whose payload is a pointer to an 8-word magicmem block, sim_control reads the args directly out of guest memory via the TLM bus, dispatches through `sim_syscall_table[]`, writes the return value back to the same memory.
- A putchar fast path at `+0x4` (the `DISPLAY_BASE_ADDR` you saw in libgloss.c)
- A command port at `+0x0` for exit/pass/fail/newline plus internal TEST_ILM/DLM/HVM hooks for cache-region tests

**Why this instead of ECALL/semihosting:**

1. **No trap handler in the guest.** Bare-metal demos have no M-mode trap vector and no privileged code. ECALL would raise an exception the guest cannot service. Semihosting (`EBREAK` + magic NOP sandwich, ARM-style) has the same problem - it requires the simulator to peek at the instruction stream around the breakpoint. HTIF needs neither: it is plain MMIO that any user-mode code can execute.

2. **Cycle-accurate Verilated CPUs are a black box from C++.** The simulator only sees AXI/AHB bus transactions coming out of the Verilated RTL. It cannot cleanly intercept an ECALL the way a functional ISS like Spike or QEMU can - by the time you observe trap state on the bus, the pipeline has already moved on. Trapping at the MMIO target is the natural seam in a TLM platform.

3. **Lineage.** This is the Berkeley/Spike/riscv-pk HTIF protocol verbatim - dev/cmd/payload packing in `command_t`, magic-memory syscall convention, tohost/fromhost. Andes inherited it because the toolchain's test infrastructure already speaks it. Replacing it with ECALL/semihosting would mean reinventing a wheel that already rolls.

4. **Multi-hart sanity.** HTIF is per-address, naturally serialised by the bus. ECALL would need per-hart privileged setup multiplied by every CPU variant in `config.yaml`.

5. **Cache visibility.** Because the host reads magicmem through the TLM memory model, the guest must flush DCache before ringing the doorbell - and libgloss.c does exactly that via `mcctlcommand`. Semihosting hides this, which is convenient but also hides real cache bugs that vsim is built to expose.

So the custom libgloss.c is not a workaround for missing semihosting - it is the matching guest half of vsim's deliberately Spike-compatible HTIF MMIO contract.

message.content: what are HTIF and MMIO ? then explain how vsim handles those  newlib POSIX syscall calls?

content.text:
## HTIF

**Host-Target Interface.** A protocol invented at UC Berkeley for the Rocket Chip / Spike RISC-V ecosystem. It lets a bare-metal guest running on a simulator (target) ask the simulator's host machine to perform services it cannot do itself - read a file, print a string, exit with a status. It is conceptually identical to ARM semihosting but uses a different mechanism: instead of trapping on `EBREAK`, the guest writes a 64-bit packed command word to two well-known memory locations called `tohost` and `fromhost`. The packet layout (from `command_t` in vsim):

```
 63    56 55    48 47                                 0
+--------+--------+------------------------------------+
| device |  cmd   |              payload               |
+--------+--------+------------------------------------+
```

`device=0, cmd=0` is the system-call channel - payload is a guest pointer to an 8-word "magic memory" block containing `{syscall_no, arg0..arg6}`. Other device numbers were originally for block devices and consoles; vsim only uses device 0.

## MMIO

**Memory-Mapped I/O.** A device is exposed to the CPU not as a special instruction but as a range of physical addresses. A normal load/store to those addresses is intercepted by the bus fabric and routed to the device instead of DRAM. The CPU does not know the difference - same `lw`/`sw` opcodes - but the bus does. In vsim this is implemented as TLM-2.0 targets attached to the AXI/AHB interconnect; each target declares a `memory_region` (base + size) and the interconnect routes transactions whose address falls in that range to that target.

## How a newlib syscall actually flows through vsim

Take `printf("hello\n")` as the concrete example. It walks through these layers:

**1. Newlib (in the guest, linked from the toolchain)**

`printf` formats into a buffer, then calls `write(1, buf, 6)`. Newlib's `write` is a thin wrapper that calls the libgloss stub `_write`.

**2. libgloss.c `_write` (the file you asked about)**

```c
ssize_t _write(int fd, const void* ptr, size_t len) {
  if (fd > 2)
    return SYSCALL_3(7, fd, ptr, len);    // file -> HTIF path
  volatile uint8_t* display = (volatile uint8_t*) DISPLAY_BASE_ADDR;
  for (size_t i = 0; i < len; ++i)
    *display = *((uint8_t*) ptr + i);     // stdout/stderr -> fast putchar
  return len;
}
```

Two completely different paths depending on `fd`. Let's follow each.

**3a. Fast path - stdout/stderr (fd 0,1,2)**

The loop does six 1-byte stores to `0xe0080004`. Each store becomes an AXI/AHB write transaction on the bus. The interconnect's address decoder matches `0xe0080004` against the sim_control region and forwards the transaction.

In `sim_control::handle_trans` (sim_control.hpp:94):
```cpp
} else if (addr == region.base_address + HTIF_PUTCHAR_OFFSET && len == 1) {
  std::putchar(buf[0]);
}
```
The byte ends up in the simulator's stdout. No HTIF protocol, no magic memory, no cache flush - just one MMIO store per character. Cheap.

**3b. HTIF path - everything else (open, read, fstat, write to a real file, exit, ...)**

`SYSCALL_3(7, fd, ptr, len)` expands to `frontend_syscall(7, fd, ptr, len, 0,0,0,0)` (from htif.h:84). That function:

```c
volatile uint64_t magicmem[8] = {n, a0, a1, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6};
sim_syscall(0, 0, (uintptr_t) magicmem);
```

It builds the 8-word magic-memory block **on the guest's own stack** in DRAM, then calls `sim_syscall(dev=0, cmd=0, tohost=&magicmem)`:

```c
flush_all_dcache();                    // <- critical
*command = TOHOST_CMD(0, 0, magicmem); // 64-bit store to 0xe0080010
asm volatile("fence rw,rw");
```

The DCache flush matters because the magic-memory array was just written by the CPU and may still be dirty in L1/L2. The host side will read it through the TLM memory model, bypassing the CPU pipeline, so it must see committed data.

Then a single 64-bit `sd` to `0xe0080010` (`HTIF_CMD_OFFSET`).

**4. Bus delivery**

The 64-bit store hits the AXI interconnect. Address decode routes it to sim_control. `handle_trans` matches `region.base_address + HTIF_CMD_OFFSET` with `len == 8` and calls `handle_htif_64bit_write`, which unpacks the 64-bit word into `command_t{device, cmd, payload}`.

(There is also a 32-bit path - two 4-byte writes at offsets +0x10 and +0x14 that get reassembled - to support `rv32` cores where a single 64-bit store is not available.)

**5. dispatch**

```cpp
void dispatch(command_t cmd) {
  uint64_t syscall_args[MAGIC_MEM_SIZE];
  auto addr_offset = cmd.payload() - data_region.region.base_address;
  auto src = data_region.data.begin() + addr_offset;
  std::copy(src, src + sizeof(syscall_args), (uint8_t*)syscall_args);
  syscall_args[0] = (this->*sim_syscall_table[syscall_args[0]])(
    syscall_args[1], ..., syscall_args[7]);
  std::copy((uint8_t*)syscall_args, ..., src);
}
```

The simulator subtracts the DRAM base from the guest pointer to index its own backing-store byte array, copies 8 words out, looks up the syscall number in `sim_syscall_table[]` (built in the constructor: 5=gettimeofday, 6=open, 7=write, 10=read, 15=fstat, ...), executes the corresponding `handle_*` method on the host (which performs a real Linux syscall using args translated through `htif/sys_call.hpp` and `htif/picolib.hpp`), and writes the return value back into `syscall_args[0]` and then back into guest DRAM at the same address.

**6. Return**

`sim_syscall` returns. `frontend_syscall` reads `magicmem[0]` - now containing the return value the host just wrote - and returns it up the stack to `_write`, which returns it to newlib, which returns it from `printf`.

The HTIF transaction is synchronous from the guest's perspective: by the time the `sd` instruction retires, sim_control has already executed the syscall and updated magicmem. There is no interrupt, no fromhost polling, no scheduling - SystemC's synchronous TLM bus call does the rendezvous for free.

## The two special cases

`_exit` does not use HTIF at all - it writes the status to `0xe0080000` (`region.base_address + 0`), which `handle_sim_command` interprets directly: values < 256 set `sim_control::exits`, which the top-level `simulator` reads and uses as the process exit code. Magic constants like `0x01234568` map to PASSED/FAILED for the test runner.

`_sbrk` is purely guest-side - it bumps a pointer above the linker symbol `_end`. The simulator is not involved because the heap lives in normal DRAM that the guest already owns.

## Summary diagram

```
guest:  printf -> newlib -> _write -> [either]
                                       |
        fast:  byte-bang 0xe0080004 ---+-> sim_control::handle_trans
                                       |     -> std::putchar
        slow:  build magicmem on stack-+
               flush DCache            |
               sd magicmem_ptr,        |
                 0xe0080010 ----------+-> handle_htif_64bit_write
                                            -> dispatch
                                                 -> read 8 words from guest DRAM
                                                 -> sim_syscall_table[7](write)
                                                    -> host ::write(...)
                                                 -> write return value back
        return magicmem[0] to caller
```

The whole design is just "guest does ordinary memory stores, the bus fabric notices they land in a magic address range, the simulator does the work on the host's behalf." No traps, no privileged mode, no instruction-stream peeking - just MMIO and a shared memory buffer.
